note: Offline successor to the v1 live-Amazon monitor task (which scored ~0.59 and depended on flaky live pages). The catalog is shipped and frozen, so the answer is deterministic. Each product has a 'listing' summary and an authoritative 'detail' page; several traps require reading the detail page and distinguishing it from the listing.

buyer_requirements.usb_c_video: USB-C must carry VIDEO via DisplayPort Alt Mode (USB-C charging/data-only does NOT count)
buyer_requirements.price_type: NEW direct purchase (used / like-new / renewed / coupon / other-seller prices do NOT count)
buyer_requirements.budget_usd: the NEW direct price must be <= 500

authority_rule: when the listing summary and the detail page disagree, the DETAIL page is authoritative.

per_product.note: Dell 27" 4K, USB-C DP Alt Mode, new, US, in stock
LG 27" 4K, USB-C 90W DP Alt Mode, new, US, in stock
ASUS ProArt 27" 4K, USB-C DP Alt Mode, new, US, in stock

per_product.detail: USB-C present but NO video (HDMI/DP only) — the classic 'USB-C charging ≠ USB-C video' trap
listing implies 4K but detail resolution is 2560x1440 (QHD)
the $429 is a used/like-new price; the NEW price is $560 (over budget) and seller is a third-party reseller
$389 is a renewed price (new $499) AND it is out of stock

scoring_anchors.authority_rule_matters: M02/M03/M05/M10 can only be rejected by reading the DETAIL page; a listing-only reading would wrongly accept them.
scoring_anchors.note: Full credit = the shortlist is exactly {M01,M04,M08} AND each rejection cites the correct trap from the detail page. Including any trap product in the shortlist is the core failure.
